name: Derry
short_description: BLM rockhounding site near Truth or Consequences featuring agate, jasper, and chalcedony in desert washes and hillsides. Free public access with 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Derry is a public BLM rockhounding area in Sierra County, New Mexico, located in the gently rolling desert terrain between Truth or Consequences and the Black Range. The site offers excellent opportunity to collect agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens that weather out of Tertiary volcanic and silica-rich deposits common throughout this region. The landscape consists of sparse creosote and yucca vegetation typical of the Chihuahuan Desert, with numerous washes and dry arroyos that expose colorful banded agates and jasper specimens.

The geological setting reflects the Basin and Range province's Tertiary volcanic history, when silica-rich fluids percolated through fractured rock and deposited microcrystalline quartz minerals. Over millions of years, erosion and weathering have broken these deposits into collectable nodules and fragments found scattered across the surface and in wash gravels.

This location is best suited for rockhounders seeking a relaxed day of surface collecting without the commitment of a fee site or long hike. You can expect to find specimens ranging from small thumb-sized pieces to occasional larger nodules. The BLM designation means no permit is required, though collection is limited to personal use only and 25 pounds per day.

Derry's proximity to Truth or Consequences makes it an accessible destination for both day trips and longer rockhounding excursions. The terrain is moderate in difficulty, with uneven ground and scattered rocks requiring sturdy footwear, but it is not so challenging as to deter families with older children or anyone with basic fitness.
history: The Derry area sits within the Basin and Range province where Tertiary volcanic activity and hydrothermal circulation deposited silica-rich fluids that formed agate and chalcedony nodules. This region was also historically prospected for silver and copper, and the same geological processes that created valuable mineral deposits also concentrated the quartz-based gems found here today.
tips: Search along dry washes and arroyo bottoms where water erosion concentrates heavier agate and jasper fragments. Look for banded patterns, translucent edges, and the waxy luster characteristic of chalcedony - even small specimens often polish beautifully. Bring a 5-gallon bucket or pack to carry finds, as carrying loose rock in pockets can be uncomfortable over a full day of walking. Early morning or late afternoon hunting reduces heat exposure and improves visibility of specimens against the soil.
terrain_notes: Derry area terrain varies from 4,200 to 4,600 feet elevation with gently rolling desert hills, sparse vegetation (creosote bush, yucca, desert grass), and numerous arroyos cutting through light tan to reddish volcanic soil. Walking distances from parking range from 0.25 to 1.5 miles depending on wash access. Footing is rocky and uneven with loose gravel common; sturdy boots are essential. No developed trails; navigation by natural washes and landmarks.
best_season: October - April. Desert temperatures from May through September regularly exceed 95F, making midday collecting uncomfortable. Winter months offer mild daytime temperatures (60-70F) and lower evaporation rates that help preserve hydration. Occasional winter rain can make arroyos temporarily impassable but also refreshes specimens.
written_directions: From Truth or Consequences, take NM-152 north toward Hillsboro for approximately 12 miles. Look for BLM access points and informal pullouts along the road or side roads heading east toward the Black Range foothills. GPS coordinates 32.788317, -107.253088 mark the general collection area. Note that road access is via local county and BLM roads that may be rough; high-clearance vehicle recommended, especially after rain.
road_conditions: Unpaved BLM and county roads, potentially rough and washboard in places. High-clearance or all-terrain vehicle recommended. Roads may be impassable immediately after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and wide spots along BLM roads near collection areas. No developed parking lot. Space for 2-6 vehicles at various access points. Park off road to avoid blocking traffic.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Surface collecting only; do not dig into intact outcrops. Pack out all trash. Respect private property boundaries - stay on BLM land.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No digging into outcrops
Pack out all trash
Respect private property boundaries
nearest_services: Truth or Consequences, approximately 12-15 miles south. Full services including gas stations, restaurants, grocery stores, and lodging.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with loose gravel and scattered rocks. No wheelchair access. Moderate walking distances (0.25-1.5 miles) over broken ground. Sturdy footwear with ankle support required. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility. Occasional steep wash banks may require scrambling. Shade is minimal; bring sun protection.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Derry, New Mexico?
faq.answer: Derry is known for agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens. Agates often display banded patterns in reds, browns, whites, and creams. Jasper is typically opaque and comes in reds and browns. These gemstones weather out of Tertiary volcanic deposits and are found scattered across the desert surface and in dry washes.

faq.question: Is there a fee or permit required to rockhound at Derry?
faq.answer: No. Derry is BLM public land with no permit required for recreational collecting. However, you are limited to 25 pounds per day for personal use only under FLPMA regulations. Commercial collection and motorized excavation are not allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Derry for rockhounding?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Daytime temperatures range from 60-75F, making full-day collecting comfortable. Summer months (May-September) often exceed 95F, creating heat exhaustion risk in the exposed desert environment. Winter rain occasionally occurs but is generally light in this region.

faq.question: How far is Derry rockhounding site from Truth or Consequences?
faq.answer: Derry is approximately 12-15 miles north of Truth or Consequences via NM-152 toward Hillsboro. Drive time is roughly 20-25 minutes. Truth or Consequences offers gas, food, lodging, and supplies, making it a convenient base for Derry rockhounding trips.

faq.question: What vehicle do I need and what are road conditions like at Derry?
faq.answer: A high-clearance or all-terrain vehicle is recommended. Access is via unpaved BLM and county roads that can be rough and washboard. Roads may be impassable after heavy rain. Passenger vehicles with good clearance may access some parking areas, but all-wheel-drive is safer for reliable access.

faq.question: Is Derry a good rockhounding site for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes. Derry is beginner-friendly with surface collecting of visible specimens and minimal climbing required. Children age 8 and up can participate comfortably. The site is dog-friendly, and the moderate terrain is accessible to most families. Bring sun protection, plenty of water, sturdy boots, and a container for your finds.

meta_title: Derry Rockhounding - New Mexico Agate & Jasper
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Derry near Truth or Consequences. Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony. No permit, 25 lb daily limit. October-April best.
